coredata.dc:title: Carbonated MOF-based graphene hydrogel for hierarchical all‑carbon supercapacitors with ultra-high areal and volumetric energy density
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhen
Ren, Jing
Bu, Jingting
Wang, Liang
Shi, Wenyan
Pan, Dengyu
Wu, Minghong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene hydrogel (GH) has been widely explored as electrode material for supercapacitors due to its three-dimensional porous network structure. However, poor conductivity and low capacitance activity have limited practical application. Herein, we design and fabricate a hierarchical all‑carbon GH-based electrode material decorated with MOF-derived porous carbon (cZIF-8) and highly N-doped graphene quantum dots. In this N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite, GH constructs an interconnected porous structure which benefits rapid electron/ion transport, and cZIF-8 provides a large specific surface area and enhances electrical conductivity, while N-GQDs contribute pseudo-capacitance and improve the wettability. The supercapacitor based on N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH shows a high areal capacitance (617.1 mF cm−2) and areal energy density (85.7 μWh cm−2) at 1 mA cm−2. It also has good rate capability (81.5% at 20 mA cm−2) and excellent cycling stability (93.1% after 10,000 cycles). Moreover, the assembled all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor can deliver a high energy density of 18.1 mWh cm−3 at high power density of 4907.3 mW cm−3. This rational-designed all‑carbon electrode opens up opportunities in the fabrication of next-generation wearable electronic equipments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene hydrogel
cZIF-8
N-doped graphene quantum dots
Areal energy density
Volumetric energy density

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) GH, (b) cZIF-8, (c) cZIF-8/GH, (d) N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH, (e-h) The SEM elemental-mapping image of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH.

(a) HRTEM images of N-GQDs (inset: The lateral size distribution of N-GQDs), (b) HRTEM image of cZIF-8/GH, (c)TEM image of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composites, (d) HRTEM image of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectra of cZIF-8, GH, cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH, (c) MB adsorption method for determination of the specific surface area.
(a) XPS survey spectra of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite, and high- resolution XPS spectrum of, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) O 1 s of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of GH, cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes at a scan rate 10 mV s−1 in 1.0 M H2SO4 electrolyte using a three-electrode system, (b) CV curves of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH at different scan rates, (c) linear relationship between current density and scan rates, (e) Capacitive and diffusion contribution of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode.

Two-electrode electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors assembled by GH, cZIF-8/GH, N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte. (a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of the different supercapacitors at a scan rate 10 mV s−1, (b) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves of the different supercapacitors at 1 mA cm−2, (c) CV curves of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH at different scan rates, (d) The specific capacitances calculated from the GCD curves of the different supercapacitors at different current densities, (e) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the supercapacitors (the inset is the EIS in the region of high frequency), (f) Cycling test of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor after 10,000 cycles at the current density of 10 mA cm−2.

Two-electrode electrochemical performance of the flexible all-solid-state of the supercapacitors fabricated by the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes in H2SO4/PVA electrolyte. (a) Cyclic voltammograms curves at various scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1, (b) Galvanostatic charge–discharge curves at different current densities, (c) The areal and volumetric capacitance at different current densities, (d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the supercapacitors (inset: an equivalent electrical circuit and the EIS in the region of high frequency), (e) Ragone plot of the supercapacitors, showing the relationship between energy density and power density at the current densities from 0.5 to 20 mA cm−2, (f) Cycling test at the current density of 5 mA cm−2.

(a, b) Schematic and digital photograph of all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor assembled by N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes, (c) CV curves of the flexible device based on N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH under different bending conditions at 50 mV s−1 (inset: the digital photographs of the flexible supercapacitor).
Schematic diagram of the rational design of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH.
Schematic illustration for the preparation process of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor.

head.ce:title: Carbonated MOF-based graphene hydrogel for hierarchical all‑carbon supercapacitors with ultra-high areal and volumetric energy density

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 201800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Prof. Z. Li, J. Ren, J. Bu, W. Shi, Shanghai Applied Radiation Institute, Shanghai

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 201800, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dr. L. Wang, Prof. D. Pan, Prof. M. Wu, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University, Shanghai 201800, P. R. China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene hydrogel (GH) has been widely explored as electrode material for supercapacitors due to its three-dimensional porous network structure. However, poor conductivity and low capacitance activity have limited practical application. Herein, we design and fabricate a hierarchical all‑carbon GH-based electrode material decorated with MOF-derived porous carbon (cZIF-8) and highly N-doped graphene quantum dots. In this N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite, GH constructs an interconnected porous structure which benefits rapid electron/ion transport, and cZIF-8 provides a large specific surface area and enhances electrical conductivity, while N-GQDs contribute pseudo-capacitance and improve the wettability. The supercapacitor based on N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH shows a high areal capacitance (617.1 mF cm−2) and areal energy density (85.7 μWh cm−2) at 1 mA cm−2. It also has good rate capability (81.5% at 20 mA cm−2) and excellent cycling stability (93.1% after 10,000 cycles). Moreover, the assembled all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor can deliver a high energy density of 18.1 mWh cm−3 at high power density of 4907.3 mW cm−3. This rational-designed all‑carbon electrode opens up opportunities in the fabrication of next-generation wearable electronic equipments.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various energy storage devices, supercapacitors have drawn much attention owing to high power density, excellent charge and discharge rate, long-term stability and low cost [1–3]. However, the energy density of supercapacitors is not their advantage in promoting practical applications. Therefore, a great deal of research effort has focused on improving the capacitances of electrode materials [4–6].

Graphene is widely used as electrode material because of its excellent electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and high theoretical electric double-layer (EDL) capacitance (550 F g−1) [7]. However, the strong π-π interaction between graphene sheets makes them readily aggregate, which leads to the decrease of surface area and capacitive performance. To address this problem, self-assembled graphene hydrogel (GH) has attracted considerable interest [8–10]. The three-dimensional framework of the GH can avoid stacking of graphene sheets. Besides, the continuous cross-linked porous network structure has a high specific surface area and provides an effective ion transport channel to promote the permeation of electrolyte [11].

Generally, GH can be easily obtained from graphene oxide (GO) dispersion which is made of reduced GO (rGO) sheets with residual oxygenated groups by hydrothermal treatment [12]. These functional groups of GH can effectively improve the capacitance of supercapacitors and enhance electrolyte wettability [13–15]. However, oxygen functional groups induce sp3 defects and distort the π conjugated system, which reduces the conductivity of GH [6,16–18]. Therefore, it is essential for GH to balance rationally oxygen functional groups and the conductivity for providing high capacitance. Recently, Fan's group [19] reported the design of oxygen clusters distributed in graphene with a “paddy land” structure to adjust the oxygen groups and the conductivity of rGO.

In recent years, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been used as self-sacrificial templates for casting porous carbon materials. Among these porous carbon materials derived from MOFs, carbonated ZIF-8 (cZIF-8) has been concerned for its high specific surface area, well-defined pore structures, chemical tunability, and good electrical conductivity [20,21]. Herein, we develop an effective technique to improve the conductivity of GH without a compromise of oxygen functional groups by adding cZIF-8 during the hydrothermal growth process of GH. The adding of cZIF-8 effectively reduces the series resistance and charge transfer resistance of GH, which leads to the improvement of capacitive performance. However, cZIF-8/GH still has a relatively low capacitance, mainly because of the EDLC mechanism of carbon materials [22,23].

The nitrogen-doped approach usually can enhance the specific capacitance, increase electron-donor capability and improve conductivity [15,22]. N-doped process can be realized via chemical vapour deposition, plasma treatment, or in the presence of other nitrogen sources [20]. These methods usually involve many tedious steps, harsh reaction conditions, and uncontrollable nitrogen content. In this paper, the small size, highly nitrogen-doped of graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as a controllable novel nitrogen resource [24–27], were easily introduced into cZIF-8/GH via electrodeposition method to form the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite. In this composite, three components play their respective roles and have a synergistic effect. The electrode based on the composite exhibits ultrahigh areal capacitance of 2247.3 mF cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2. The electrode of the assembled all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor exhibits excellent high rate performance with a capacitance of 1234.2 mF cm−2 (636.1 F cm−3) at 1 mA cm−2. The energy density of the device is 70.5 μWh cm−2 at the power density of 0.5 mW cm−2 and the capacitance retention rate can reach 90.03% after 5000 cycles.

Zn(NO3)2•6H2O, Methanol, PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone), and 2-methylimidazole were obtained from Sinopharm Group. Other chemicals used in the experiment were available from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All raw chemicals do not need additional purification.

cZIF-8 was synthesized according to previously reported method [23]. Firstly, the prepared cZIF-8 was weighed and ground thoroughly, then poured into a vial with GO solution, and GO was prepared by a modified method [28]. After that, the mixed solution was ultrasonicated to be evenly dispersed, and hydrothermally reacted in a Teflon reaction kettle at 170 °C for 1 h. Cooling to room temperature, cZIF-8/GH composite was obtained.

Previously we reported the synthesis of N-GQDs via molecular fusion route [24]. 4 g of pyrene was slowly added to 320 mL of concentrated HNO3 solution, and then reacted at 80 °C and condensed at reflux for 48 h. The product was cooled to room temperature. A microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm was used for filtration. During the filtration process, deionized water is repeatedly added until the pH of the solution is about 7. The resulting solution is the precursor solution required for the experiment. 4 mL of hydrazine hydrate was slowly added into 40 mL of the prepared precursor solution, and hydrothermal reacted in a Teflon reaction kettle at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the N-GQD solution was obtained through suction filtration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: 40 mL N-GQD solution was poured into a beaker as an electrolyte, and the prepared cZIF-8/GH pressed on the titanium mesh acted as a working electrode with the Pt foil electrode as the counter electrode. The electrodeposition process was performed under different potential and electro-deposition time. The resulting product was washed with deionized water, and final composite on the titanium mesh was prepared. Finally, the titanium mesh loading with N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH was cut to produce the electrode for the supercapacitor.

The preparation of H2SO4/PVA gel electrolyte was performed according to the references [23]. The prepared N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode material was pressed on the PET film, and the copper foil was stuck on the electrode surface by conductive paste. Finally, two electrodes which were uniformly coated with gel electrolyte were assembled using filter paper as a separator, and an all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor was prepared.

The morphology of the materials was investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-7500F, 15 kV) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100F, operated at 120 kV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were measured by a Rigaku DMAX2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy was obtained by setting the excitation wavelength to 785 nm on a Renishaw laser Raman spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer.

The surface area of the wet electrode was usually measured by a methylene blue (MB) adsorption method [29]. Firstly, three samples (1 mg GH, cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH) were respectively put in the aqueous solution of MB (mass fraction 0.2%, 0.1%, 0.01%, 0.004%) under continuous stirring. Then, the obtained solution was analyzed by a UV spectrometer (SPEC ORD-210) with the absorbance value of λmax = 665±1 nm. The SA of each sample calculated according to the equations: Γ = (CoCe) V/m, SA = Γ∞NAA; where, Γ is adsorption amount of MB, Co and Ce are respectively initial concentration and equilibrium concentration of MB, V is the volume of MB solution, m is the amount of adsorbent. Γ∞ is a saturated adsorption capacity, NA is Avogadro constant, and A is 2.54 m2 in this experiment.

All electrochemical tests including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Shanghai, China). Firstly, in the three-electrode system, N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH, Ag/AgCl electrode, and Pt line were used as the working electrode, the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The potential window of the three-electrode system was −0.2 to 0.8 V, which used 1 M H2SO4 as an electrolyte. The capacitance can calculate by using Eq. (1).
(1)
C single electrode
= ∫ I
dV / υAΔV
where I (A) is the current relating to the voltage V, △V (V) is the voltage window, υ (mV s−1) is the scan rate, and A is the area of electrode.

Secondly, in the two-electrode system, using 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution and H2SO4/PVA as the electrolyte respectively, the CV test was measured at different sweep speeds and GCD measured at different current densities. The EIS measurement is in the frequency range of 0.01 to 106 Hz. The Eqs. (2), and (3) were used to calculate the areal, and volumetric capacitance of the device [30]. Meanwhile, the energy density and power density of the supercapacitor were calculated by using Eqs. (4) and (5).
(2)
C A
=
I discharge
×
∆ t
discharge
∆ V × A
(3)
C V
=
C A
t
(4)
E =
C ×
∆ V
2
×
10 3
2 × 3600
(5)
P =
3600 × E
∆ t
discharge
where I
discharge is the applied discharge current, △t
discharge is the discharge time, A and t are the area and thickness of the total device, △V is the device operating potential. The thickness of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode is 20.3 μm (Fig. S1), and the areal mass loading is 4.1 mg cm−2.

A schematic diagram describes the rational design of these three materials to construct N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode material with high conductivity, high capacitive activity, and excellent electrochemical performance for supercapacitors (Scheme. 1 ). In this hybrid and hierarchical construction, interconnected porous microstructure of GH allows electrolytes to freely diffuse inside and through the network, and can be directly used as binder-free supercapacitor electrodes. cZIF-8 provides a large specific surface area and enhances electrical conductivity, therefore it is introduced to form cZIF-8/GH to improve the poor conductivity of GH caused by the oxygen-containing functional groups. To boost the electrochemical activity and enhance the surface wettability of the electrode, N-GQDs are deposited on the surface of cZIF-8/GH by electrochemical deposition to form the N-GQD @ cZIF-8/GH electrode, which has high performance because of electric double layer capacitance mainly provided by the cZIF-8/GH network and pseudocapacitance by N-GQDs.

The preparation process of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor is illustrated in Scheme. 2 . Firstly, the cZIF-8/GH hydrogel was formed by cZIF-8 and GO mixed solution via hydrothermal reaction. Using the prepared cZIF-8/GH pressed on the titanium mesh as a working electrode, the Pt foil as a counter electrode, N-GQD solution as an electrolyte, N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode was prepared by loading N-GQDs on cZIF-8/GH via electrochemical deposition. Finally, two electrodes were assembled to form the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor.

SEM and TEM were used to characterize the morphology of composite materials. The SEM image of pure GH is an interconnected 3D porous network with pore sizes ranging from sub-micrometres to several micrometres (Fig. 1a). The geometric shape of cZIF-8 appears as a dodecahedron with a diameter of 80 nm approximately. (Fig. 1b). As shown in Fig. 1c, and Fig. S2, while retaining the 3D interlinked pore structure of GH, the cZIF-8 nanocrystals are dispersed well to form cZIF-8/GH composite through the hydrothermal reaction. After adding N-GQDs (Fig. 1d and Fig. S3), it can be seen that the N-GQDs are well distributed and simultaneously the 3D interlinked porous structure is in good keep, which suggests the addition of highly capacitive N-GQDs does not impede electron and ion transport benefiting from the hierarchical porous structure. Elemental composition analysis of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH obtained from EDX shows that the distribution of C, N, and O elements is relatively uniform (Fig. 1e-h).

TEM images (Fig. 2a) show that the prepared N-GQDs have an average diameter of 2 nm approximately with the d-spacing of the (100) crystal plane (0.21 nm). Fig. 2b exhibits dodecahedron cZIF-8 nanocrystals are well distributed on the lamella of GH to form a cZIF-8/GH composite. Fig. 2c clearly shows that N-GQDs are on the surface of cZIF-8 and GH. The HRTEM image of the composite (Fig. 2d) clearly shows the adjacent interlayer distances of GH (0.38 nm), cZIF-8 (0.34 nm), and N-GQDs (0.21 n) respectively, which is further demonstrated that the three components are well combined.

The structure of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH composite was characterized by XRD, Raman, solid-liquid adsorption and XPS measurements. As shown in Fig. 3a, XRD pattern of GH exhibits a broad peak centred at 2θ = 24.1°, corresponding to the graphitic (002) profile with an interlayer spacing of 3.8 Å. This value is higher than that of cZIF-8 (3.4 Å), which may account for the presence of residual oxygenated functional groups in the GH. Compared with GH, the diffraction peaks of cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH are slightly up-shifted with an interlayer spacing of 3.6 Å and 3.5 Å because of the combination with cZIF-8 and N-GQDs.

Raman spectra of GH, cZIF-8, cZIF-8/GH, N-GQD @ cZIF-8/GH (Fig. 3b) show a typical D band and a G band located at 1344 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, with the I D/I G ratio of 1.09, 0.93, 1.05, and 1.04, respectively. The I D/I G of GH shows a high degree of disorder due to oxygen-containing functional groups. After the adding of cZIF-8 and N-GQDs, I D/I G decreases, which contributes to the improvement of conductivity.

The surface area (SA) of wet electrodes was evaluated by the MB adsorption method to reveal the real state of ion adsorption [10,29,31]. As shown in the Fig. 3c, the S A of GH, cZIF-8/GH, and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH are 345.4 m2 g−1, 563.6 m2 g−1, 431.3 m2 g−1, respectively. Compared to GH, the S A of cZIF-8/GH is markedly increased owing to the high specific surface area of cZIF-8. After the deposition of N-GQDs, the S A of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH decreases. However, it is still larger than GH, indicating that the 3D interconnected porous structure still retains, and N-GQDs is evenly distributed inside.

XPS measurement was used to detect the elemental composition and bonding state of C, N, and O atoms in N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH. As displayed in Fig. 4a, the contents of C, N, and O are 84.8%, 9.3%, and 5.89% respectively, corresponding to C1s peak at 284 eV, N1s peak at 399 eV, and O1s peak at 531 eV. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4b) has the following carbon bonds: 284.1 eV (C=C), 286.1 eV (CO), 287.1 eV (CN), 288.3 eV (C=O/O=C-O) [32–34]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4c) can be divided into three peaks, including pyrrole-N/pyridine-N (399.2 eV), graphitic-N (400.8 eV), and oxidized-N (401.1 eV) [35]. The presence of pyrrole-N/pyridone-N is beneficial to improve the electrochemical activity caused by redox reactions during the charging and discharging. Graphitic N atoms embedded into the graphene layer enhance the conductivity. Therefore, the adding of N-GQDs can provide additional pseudo-capacitance and improve EDLC performance for supercapacitors. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows two separated peaks, including 530.2 eV (C-OH) and 532.1 eV (C=O) [36–39], which is beneficial to the wettability and pseudo-capacitance.

The CV measurements of the GH, cZIF-8/GH and N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes were tested at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 over a potential range of −0.2 to 0.8 V with 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte in a three-electrode system. Fig. S4a-b shows that the performance of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode is strongly dependent on appropriate preparation conditions such as cZIF-8 quality (0.5 mg), deposition potential (3 V), and deposition time (2 h). In Fig. 5a, the CV curve of cZIF-8 and cZIF-8/GH electrode exhibits a nearly rectangular shape, indicating a typical EDLC behaviour, and the addition of cZIF-8 is beneficial to improve the double layer capacitance of GH. N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode exhibits two pairs of redox peaks contributed by pyrrole-N/pyridone-N (0.32/0.29 V, V = 0.03 V) and quinine/hydroquinone (0.46/0.05 V, V = 0.41 V). These reversible redox reactions (expressed as Eqs. (1)–(3)) [40] make the capacitance of the composite (2444.3 mF cm−2) nearly twice higher than that of cZIF-8/GH (1224.1 mF cm−2), which are calculated by CV curves.

Fig. 5b shows CV curves of the final composite electrode at different scan rates, which also provides insight into the charging/discharging kinetics by the relationship between the peak current (i) and sweep rate (v). The relationship is expresses as i = a v b [41,42], and the b value provides information about the charge storage mechanism. For a redox reaction caused by semi-infinite diffusion limits insight, such as battery material, b value is about 0.5. While for a capacitive process, such as capacitor material, b value is about 1. As shown in Fig. 5c, the peak current varies as sweep rate with b ≈ 1, indicating of a capacitive energy storage mechanism. An analysis of the voltammetry sweep data was used to analyze the contribution ratio of two kinds of capacitors, which contributes to the total stored charge [43,44]. By dividing the current response i at the fixed potential V into capacitive effects (K 1 v) and diffusion-controlled reactions (K 2 v 1/2): i(V) = K 1 v + K 2 v 1/2, the ratio of the capacitance contribution can be further quantified. In Fig. S5a-b, the capacitance calculated based on the area of the red zone is compared with the total capacitance obtained from the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrode at different scan rates (2, 10, 20, 30, and 50 mV s−1). Fig. 5d shows the different proportion of distribution attributed to the two different charge storage mechanisms. The diffusion capacitance of the electrode can reach up to 62% at the scan rate of 2 mV s−1, which is a distinct contribution from diffusion-controlled processes. When the scan rate increases to 50 mV s−1, the surface capacitance contribution can reach a maximum value of 78%. It is noteworthy that the diffusion-controlled contribution still retains 22% at a high scan rate, suggesting good rate capability of the electrode owing to active redox sites.

A series of symmetric supercapacitors were assembled with 1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution as the electrolyte. The CV (Fig. 6a), and GCD curves (Fig. 6b) show a better electrochemical performance for N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor comparing with GH and cZIF-8/GH supercapacitors. Fig. 6c shows the shapes of the CV curves for N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor are no visible change even at a high scan rate of 100 mV s−1, indicating the electrolyte ion can efficiently transport throughout the electrode. The specific capacitances calculated from the GCD curves are shown in Fig. 6d. The N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor can deliver an ultrahigh areal capacitance of 617.1 mF cm−2 (75.7 F g−1) at a current density of 1 mA cm−2, which is around 1.3 times higher than that of the cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor (457.8 mF cm−2). Moreover, the composite electrode has a good rate capability, with 81.5% capacitance retention from 1 to 20 mA cm−2.

We have further investigated the ion transport properties for supercapacitors using EIS measurement. The Nyquist plots of GH, cZIF-8/GH, N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitors are shown in Fig. 6e. The equivalent series resistance (R s) and charge-transfer resistance (R ct) of the device based on GH, cZIF-8/GH, N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitors are 1.93, 0.58, 0.69 Ω and 1.10, 0.52, 0.71 Ω, respectively. It's clear that the adding of cZIF-8 effectively reduces R s and R ct of GH, which is caused by the increasing of the electron transport area and the conductivity of cZIF-8. These resistances increase only slightly after the integration with N-GQDs, which is because the N-doping and oxygen functional groups of GQDs facilitate the penetration of the electrolyte into the electrode material [45]. The cycling test of the supercapacitor which is prepared from the final composite shows 93.1% capacitance retention and 100% coulombic efficiency over 10,000 cycles at 10 mA cm−2 (Fig. 6f), demonstrating excellent electrochemical stability of the device. Besides, Fig. 6f shows saddle drops in capacity which is consistent with the characteristic of GH material [46]. The SEM image and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of the N-GQD@ZIF-8/GH electrode material before and after 10,000 cycles are shown in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9, which indicates that the N-GQD@ZIF-8/GH has good stability.

The N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor has a high energy density of 85.7 μWh cm−2(10.5 Wh kg−1) with a power density of 0.5 mW cm−2 (500.2 W kg−1)at 1 mA cm−2, as shown in Fig. S6, which is much higher than that of GH (43.8 μWh cm−2), cZIF-8/GH (63.6 μWh cm−2), and also other previously reported GH-based supercapacitors (Table. S1). The comparison of the gravimetric and volumetric energy storage performance of N-GQD@ZIF-8/GH supercapacitor with other typical carbon-based supercapacitors is shown in Table S2 and Table S3. To further demonstrate the practical application of the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor, three connected devices can power a commercial red LED (3 V). Compared to a single battery (1.0 V) at the same current density, the series device with a 3.0 V potential window (Fig. S7a) shows an almost same charge/discharge time. (Fig. S7b).

All-solid-state flexible supercapacitors were assembled using H2SO4/PVA gel as the solid electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 7a, the CV curves maintain the shape without apparent distortion even at a high scan rate of 100 mV s−1, indicating an excellent rate performance of the device. Fig. 7b shows the charge/discharge curves at different current densities, which have approximately symmetrical shapes. The specific capacitance of the device is 530.9 mF cm−2 (130.8 F cm−3) at 1 mA cm−2 and remains 391.8 mF cm−2 (96.1 F cm−3) at 20 mA cm−2 (Fig. 7c), which has a capacitance retention rate of 73.8%. The Nyquist plot of the device is shown in Fig. 7d, where the equivalent circuit is provided including the series resistance (R s), charge-transfer resistance (R ct) and the Warburg impedance (R w). As can be observed, in the high frequency region, the device has a low R s (2.41 Ω) and R ct (3.27 Ω), indicating that electrons and ions transfer quickly at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Moreover, the line slope of the device is very precipitous, revealing the good accessibility for electrolyte diffusion. As shown in Fig. 7e, the device exhibits an extremely high energy density of 18.1 mWh cm−3 at the power density of 255.5 mW cm−3 and retains a remarkable energy density of 12.8 mWh cm−3 even at an ultrahigh power density of 4907.3 mW cm−3, which is much higher than previously reported GH-based all-solid-state supercapacitors, such as PA/GH (7.98 mWh cm−3 at 25 mW cm−3) [30], CPGH (8.8 mWh cm−3 at 30.8 mW cm−3) [46], GH/Fe-MOF(0.035 mWh cm−3 at 3 mW cm−3) [47], GHF(4.6 mWh cm−3 at 16 mW cm−3) [48], PANI/GH(13.2 mWh cm−3 at 2427.4 mW cm−3) [49]. Fig. 7f shows the device has a high retention ratio of 90.03% after 5000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2.

Fig. 8a shows a schematic diagram of the assembling process of the all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor with two GQD@cZIF-8/GH electrodes pressed onto PET film using PVA/H2SO4 hydrogel as the electrolyte. The digital photograph of the device is shown in Fig. 8b. The flexibility of the practical all-solid supercapacitor was further evaluated. Fig. 8c shows the CV curves of the flexible device at different bending degrees of 0°, 60°, 90°, 120°, 180° (Fig. 8c inset). There is almost no obvious change, indicating excellent bending ability and stability of the flexible device.

In summary, a high areal and volumetric capacitance supercapacitor has been reasonably designed and assembled. In this composite of the electrode, interconnected porous microstructure of GH allows electrolytes to freely diffuse inside and through the network, and can be directly used as binder-free supercapacitor electrodes. cZIF-8 with excellent conductivity provides a large specific surface area, and N-GQDs provide more active sites and improve the surface wettability of the composite. The synergy of the three components makes the N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH supercapacitor possess high energy density, high power density and long cycling life. The assembled all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor which has excellent cycling stability and flexibility can deliver a high energy density of 18.1 mWh cm−3 at the power density of 4907.3 mW cm−3. The hierarchical porous carbon structure of N-GQD@cZIF-8/GH has a better application prospects.

Z. Li supervised the project, conceived and designed all the experiments. J. Ren realized the material synthesis and electrochemical measurement. J.T. Bu, W.Y.Shi and L. Wang performed the characterizations of the material. D. Pan and M. Wu discussed the results and analyzed the data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11975148, 11775138), and Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13078).
